body:
The vivid case, and the one that makes §24 legible to a person rather than to a spec reader. §23 built most of the path: §23.3 decided a sandboxed renderer writes under a per-renderer granted author into the SANDBOX POOL, never canonical, and §23.9 built the confinement. §24.1 supplied the pool. What §24.7 adds is the FRAME.

BUILD, in the three parts §24.7 names:
FIRST, A CORRECTION TO CARRY INTO THE WORK: §24.7's prose says 'the stock React host (§23.2)', and that phrase does not map onto built code. There is no React host component in Loam. §23.2 is the host CONTRACT — the capability-scoped handles injected into a render function — and the serving path is `serveRouteImpl` in `src/gateway/renderers.ts`, which runs the bundle in the §23.9 worker and returns rendered output. So 'the frame' is chrome the SERVING PATH wraps around a quarantined route's output, not a React component to write. Build it there, and amend §24.7's wording in the same PR so the spec stops describing a host that does not exist.

1. HANDLES SCOPED TO THE POOL — the serving path, mounting a quarantined renderer, injects handles whose writes land IN the quarantine (§23.3's pen-into-pool) and whose reads see `primary-ground ⊎ its-own-writes` (§24.2). The point is that a probationary app is genuinely RUNNING, statefully: the operator watches it DO things, not paint a frozen preview.
2. A VISIBLY-SEQUESTERED FRAME — chrome that says without ambiguity: this is probation; its writes are LIVE HERE and quarantined from your store; promotion is the only crossing. §24.7 is emphatic about the failure mode: the frame must NOT say 'its writes go nowhere.' That would be a LIE — they go into the pool, and that is the whole point of a dry-run. A user must be fooled neither into thinking a probationary face is load-bearing NOR into thinking it is inert.
3. PROMOTION CONTROLS AT THE FRAME'S EDGE — bless the law (§24.4 / T33), adopt an output (§24.3, built #111). The only door out, and it is visible.

RAILS:
- A write from the quarantined renderer lands in the POOL and is absent from the primary, asserted at the store level, not the UI level.
- The app reads its own write back (it is stateful and live, not a preview) — this is the assertion that distinguishes §24.7 from a screenshot.
- Dropping the pool while the frame is mounted is consequence-free, and the frame says so.
- The frame's copy is itself under test for the §24.7 lie: assert the sequestration language does not claim writes go nowhere. (A string test is weak, but the failure mode IS a copy failure, so test the copy.)
- Erasure reaches through the mounted frame (§24.8 holds while a renderer is live, not only at rest).

THE VILLAGE (CLAUDE.md step 8): extend `demos/village/phase-quarantine.mjs` or add a new act so the village RUNS a stranger's face against real ground, writes into the pool, shows the frame, promotes one output, and drops the rest — end-to-end and visible. Update the demonstration ledger in `demos/village/README.md`. This is the act that shows what the whole §24/§27 arc was for; make it ambitious.

CARE: renderer + capability surface → Myk's merge (P6).
title: §24.7: the sequestered frame — a stranger's app running against your real ground, visibly on probation
